labs.clinical_note: White blood cell count is normal.
Red blood cell count is normal.
Red blood cell size is normal.
Hemoglobin content per red cell is normal.
Red cell size variation is normal.
Magnesium is at the lower end of normal. Optimal magnesium supports insulin sensitivity.
Fasting glucose is elevated above normal, indicating your body is not processing blood sugar as efficiently as optimal.
HbA1c is elevated, indicating your average blood sugar over the past 2-3 months has been higher than optimal.
Total cholesterol is within desirable range.
HDL cholesterol is below the recommended level for females. Higher HDL provides better cardiovascular protection.
Triglycerides are elevated, often linked to how your body processes carbohydrates and insulin resistance.
This ratio is at the upper end of optimal.
Fasting insulin is in the upper portion of normal, indicating your pancreas is working harder than optimal.
HOMA-IR is significantly elevated, indicating reduced sensitivity to insulin at the cellular level.
Vitamin D is insufficient. Optimal vitamin D supports hormonal balance, insulin sensitivity, and immune function.
Vitamin B12 is within normal range, important for vegetarians to maintain.
DHEA-S is elevated above normal range. This adrenal androgen contributes to the overall androgen excess pattern.
Estradiol is within normal range for the follicular phase.
LH is in the upper portion of normal. The LH:FSH ratio of approximately 3:1 is elevated.
Total testosterone is elevated above the normal range for females.
Free testosterone is elevated, indicating more bioavailable androgen activity.
SHBG is below normal. Low SHBG allows more free testosterone to circulate, amplifying androgen effects.
AMH is elevated, reflecting increased ovarian follicle count typical of polycystic ovarian morphology.

diagnoses.clinical_rationale: Classic hormonal pattern: elevated total testosterone (78 ng/dL), elevated free testosterone (8.2 pg/mL), elevated DHEA-S (380 µg/dL), and suppressed SHBG (16 nmol/L). LH:FSH ratio of approximately 3:1 is elevated. AMH of 6.8 ng/mL is consistent with polycystic ovarian morphology. Family history of PCOS in sister supports genetic predisposition. Mother's hypothyroidism increases vigilance for thyroid involvement. Patient expressed interest in fertility, making early intervention important.

Fasting glucose 104 mg/dL and HbA1c 5.8% meet pre-diabetes criteria. HOMA-IR of 4.8 confirms significant insulin resistance. Fasting insulin at 18.5 µIU/mL is at upper normal, indicating compensatory hyperinsulinemia. Low SHBG (16 nmol/L) is driven by hyperinsulinemia. Dyslipidemia pattern (low HDL 44 mg/dL, elevated triglycerides 168 mg/dL) is characteristic. Family history of Type 2 Diabetes in father and paternal grandmother, plus Asian genetic lineage, increases metabolic risk. Insulin resistance is central to PCOS pathophysiology.

Vitamin D at 22 ng/mL is below the 30 ng/mL threshold for sufficiency. Vitamin D insufficiency is highly prevalent in PCOS and worsens both insulin resistance and hormonal imbalance. Sedentary desk job likely limits sun exposure. Repletion is important for metabolic and reproductive health.

foods_to_eat.food: Leafy greens — spinach, Swiss chard, kale (espinaca, acelga, col rizada)
foods_to_eat.why_it_helps: Very low glycemic impact with high magnesium content to support insulin sensitivity. Rich in folate for reproductive health. Suitable for vegetarian diet.

foods_to_eat.quantity_frequency: 2 tablespoons ground flaxseed and 2 tablespoons chia daily
foods_to_eat.why_it_helps: Flaxseed contains lignans that help reduce free testosterone and support healthy estrogen metabolism. Both provide fiber that slows glucose absorption and omega-3 ALA for inflammation. Excellent for vegetarians.
Low glycemic legumes provide plant protein and soluble fiber that stabilize blood sugar. Rich in iron and B vitamins important for vegetarians. Widely available throughout Latin America.

foods_to_eat.food: Fatty fish — salmon, sardines, mackerel (salmón, sardinas, caballa)
foods_to_eat.quantity_frequency: 3-4 servings per week, 4-6 oz per serving (if you eat fish)
foods_to_eat.why_it_helps: EPA and DHA omega-3s reduce inflammation (lowering hs-CRP), improve insulin sensitivity, and may help regulate menstrual cycles. If strictly vegetarian, emphasize algae-based omega-3 supplements.
Studies specifically in PCOS show walnuts improve lipid profiles, reduce androgens, and increase SHBG. Excellent vegetarian protein and fat source.

foods_to_eat.quantity_frequency: 1/2 to 1 teaspoon daily in foods or beverages
foods_to_eat.why_it_helps: Ceylon cinnamon improves insulin sensitivity and helps stabilize blood sugar after meals. Studies show benefit specifically in PCOS.
Clinical studies show spearmint tea reduces free testosterone and improves hirsutism in PCOS. A simple, accessible intervention with anti-androgen properties.
Monounsaturated fats help raise HDL cholesterol (currently low at 44 mg/dL) and improve insulin sensitivity without spiking blood sugar.
Complete plant protein with moderate glycemic impact. Provides magnesium, iron, and B vitamins important for vegetarians and hormonal health.

foods_to_eat.food: Turmeric with black pepper (cúrcuma con pimienta negra)
foods_to_eat.quantity_frequency: 1 teaspoon turmeric with black pepper daily
foods_to_eat.why_it_helps: Curcumin is anti-inflammatory (your hs-CRP is elevated at 2.1) and has been studied in PCOS for improving insulin sensitivity and reducing androgen levels.

foods_to_eat.food: Berries — blueberries, strawberries, blackberries (arándanos, fresas, moras)
foods_to_eat.why_it_helps: Low glycemic fruits rich in polyphenols that improve insulin sensitivity. Anthocyanins reduce inflammation.
High in zinc, which supports healthy hormone production and insulin function. Also provides magnesium and plant protein.

foods_to_eat.food: Coconut yogurt with live cultures (yogur de coco con probióticos)
foods_to_eat.why_it_helps: Dairy-free probiotic source (respecting lactose intolerance). Gut microbiome health influences hormone metabolism and inflammation.

foods_to_eat.quantity_frequency: 1/2 cup cooked, 3-4 times per week, or as tea
foods_to_eat.why_it_helps: Traditional remedy with demonstrated blood sugar-lowering effects. Contains compounds that mimic insulin action. Commonly used in Latin American and Asian cuisines.

foods_to_eat.food: Extra virgin olive oil (aceite de oliva extra virgen)
foods_to_eat.why_it_helps: Anti-inflammatory monounsaturated fats support hormonal health and improve lipid profile.

foods_to_avoid.food: Refined carbohydrates — white rice, white bread, refined flour products (arroz blanco, pan blanco, productos de harina refinada)
foods_to_avoid.reduction_target: Limit to 1 small serving daily maximum; replace with whole grains
foods_to_avoid.why_to_avoid: Rapidly spike blood sugar and insulin, worsening insulin resistance and suppressing SHBG further. Your HOMA-IR of 4.8 indicates your cells are already struggling to respond to insulin.

foods_to_avoid.food: Sweetened beverages — sodas, packaged juices, sweetened lassi alternatives (refrescos, jugos envasados)
foods_to_avoid.why_to_avoid: Liquid sugars cause rapid blood sugar and insulin spikes. Fructose specifically worsens insulin resistance and raises triglycerides (yours elevated at 168 mg/dL).
You have reported lactose intolerance. Additionally, dairy can stimulate insulin and IGF-1, potentially worsening PCOS symptoms. Use plant-based alternatives.

foods_to_avoid.food: High-glycemic fruits in excess — mango, papaya, pineapple, watermelon
foods_to_avoid.reduction_target: Limit to 1/2 cup serving, paired with protein or fat
foods_to_avoid.why_to_avoid: While natural, these fruits cause significant blood sugar spikes when eaten alone or in large quantities. Pair with nuts or seeds to slow absorption.
Create inflammatory compounds and typically use omega-6 rich oils. Your hs-CRP of 2.1 indicates elevated inflammation that worsens PCOS.

foods_to_avoid.food: Commercial baked goods and sweets (galletas, pasteles, dulces)
foods_to_avoid.why_to_avoid: Combination of refined flour, sugar, and unhealthy fats maximally spikes insulin and promotes inflammation.

foods_to_avoid.food: Vegetable oils high in omega-6 — corn, soybean, sunflower oil (aceite de maíz, soya, girasol)
foods_to_avoid.reduction_target: Replace with olive oil or coconut oil for cooking
foods_to_avoid.why_to_avoid: Excess omega-6 promotes inflammation. Switch to olive oil or coconut oil to support anti-inflammatory balance.

foods_to_avoid.reduction_target: Limit to 1-2 cups coffee or tea daily; avoid caffeine after 2pm
foods_to_avoid.why_to_avoid: Excess caffeine can elevate cortisol and worsen sleep quality. You report only 5-6 hours of sleep, which already impairs insulin sensitivity.

foods_to_avoid.food: Instant noodles and highly processed vegetarian foods
foods_to_avoid.why_to_avoid: High in refined carbohydrates, sodium, and often contain inflammatory additives. Focus on whole food vegetarian options.
Many plant milks contain added sugars that contribute to blood sugar instability. Always select unsweetened varieties.

exercise_recommendations.why_it_helps: Building muscle is the most effective intervention for improving insulin sensitivity. Each pound of muscle added increases glucose uptake capacity. Strength training also helps regulate androgen levels and supports healthy body composition. Your HOMA-IR of 4.8 will respond significantly to increased muscle mass.
exercise_recommendations.safety_notes: Start with bodyweight exercises or light weights. You already do yoga, which provides foundation for body awareness and core strength.

exercise_recommendations.why_it_helps: Studies specifically in PCOS show HIIT improves insulin sensitivity, reduces androgens, and helps regulate menstrual cycles more effectively than steady-state cardio. Also time-efficient for busy schedules.
exercise_recommendations.safety_notes: Start with longer recovery intervals (1:2 work-to-rest ratio) and progress as fitness improves.

exercise_recommendations.exercise_type: Daily Movement — walking, yoga, or light activity
exercise_recommendations.duration: 20-30 minutes after main meals; continue yoga practice
exercise_recommendations.why_it_helps: Post-meal walking reduces blood sugar spikes by 20-30%. Your existing yoga practice supports stress reduction (lowering cortisol) and body awareness. Given your sedentary desk job, regular movement breaks are essential.

supplement_recommendations.dosage_range: 4,000-5,000 IU daily for 8-12 weeks, then reassess
supplement_recommendations.timing: With a fat-containing meal for absorption
supplement_recommendations.why_it_helps: Your vitamin D at 22 ng/mL is insufficient. Studies show vitamin D supplementation in PCOS improves insulin sensitivity, reduces androgens, and supports fertility. Target level is 40-60 ng/mL.
supplement_recommendations.safety_note: No medication interactions. You take Biotin and Vitamin C, which are compatible.
supplement_recommendations.local_availability_note: Vitamin D3 (Vitamina D3) is widely available in pharmacies throughout Latin America.

supplement_recommendations.timing: Divided into 2 doses, morning and evening
supplement_recommendations.why_it_helps: Inositol is the most studied supplement for PCOS with strong evidence. It improves insulin sensitivity (addressing your HOMA-IR of 4.8), reduces androgens, regulates menstrual cycles, and supports fertility. The 40:1 ratio mimics the body's natural ratio.
supplement_recommendations.safety_note: No medication interactions. Very well tolerated. Compatible with your current supplements.
supplement_recommendations.local_availability_note: Inositol is available in health food stores and some pharmacies in larger Latin American cities. May need to be ordered online.

supplement_recommendations.supplement_name: Omega-3 (EPA + DHA) — from fish oil or algae
supplement_recommendations.why_it_helps: Reduces inflammation (your hs-CRP is elevated at 2.1), improves insulin sensitivity, and helps raise HDL cholesterol (currently low at 44 mg/dL). Studies in PCOS show improvement in triglycerides and hormonal markers.
supplement_recommendations.safety_note: No interactions with your current supplements. If strictly vegetarian, choose algae-based omega-3.
supplement_recommendations.local_availability_note: Fish oil widely available; algae-based omega-3 available in health food stores.

supplement_recommendations.why_it_helps: Magnesium supports insulin signaling and blood sugar regulation. Your serum level is at the lower end of normal (1.8 mg/dL). Also supports sleep quality (you report only 5-6 hours) and stress management.
supplement_recommendations.safety_note: No interactions. May improve sleep quality, hence evening timing.
supplement_recommendations.local_availability_note: Magnesium Glycinate available in health food stores. Magnesium Citrate is a common pharmacy alternative.

supplement_recommendations.why_it_helps: Activates AMPK to improve insulin sensitivity. Studies show similar efficacy to metformin for blood sugar and has been studied specifically in PCOS. Your elevated HOMA-IR, fasting glucose, and HbA1c indicate clear benefit.
supplement_recommendations.safety_note: Can lower blood sugar, so monitor for symptoms of low blood sugar. Start with 500 mg once daily and increase gradually.
supplement_recommendations.local_availability_note: Available in health food stores (tiendas naturistas) in Latin America.

patient_summary: Your lab panel reviewed 44 markers across blood, metabolic, nutritional, and hormonal systems, with 9 values outside normal range concentrated in your reproductive hormones and metabolic markers. Your hormonal pattern — elevated testosterone (total 78 ng/dL, free 8.2 pg/mL), elevated DHEA-S (380 µg/dL), low SHBG (16 nmol/L), elevated LH:FSH ratio, and elevated AMH (6.8 ng/mL) — is consistent with polycystic ovary syndrome, which your sister also has. This pattern is driven by underlying insulin resistance, confirmed by your elevated HOMA-IR (4.8), fasting glucose (104 mg/dL), and HbA1c (5.8%), placing you in the pre-diabetic range. Your vitamin D at 22 ng/mL is insufficient and worsens both the insulin resistance and hormonal imbalance. The most impactful first steps are starting inositol (myo-inositol + D-chiro-inositol) and vitamin D3 supplementation, adding resistance training 3-4 times weekly, and shifting your vegetarian diet toward low-glycemic whole foods while reducing refined carbohydrates. With consistent adherence over 3-4 months, improvement in insulin sensitivity, hormonal balance, and lipid markers is achievable, and repeat testing at the 3-month mark is recommended.

system_summaries.summary: All complete blood count markers are within normal range. Your hemoglobin (12.8 g/dL) is adequate, which is important to monitor as a vegetarian. No signs of anemia or immune abnormalities.
Kidney function is normal and electrolytes are well-balanced. Magnesium at 1.8 mg/dL is at the lower end of normal — optimizing magnesium intake supports insulin sensitivity and sleep quality.
All liver markers are within normal range. Liver function is healthy.

Your metabolic markers indicate insulin resistance. Fasting glucose (104 mg/dL) and HbA1c (5.8%) are in the pre-diabetic range. HOMA-IR of 4.8 confirms your cells are not responding efficiently to insulin. This drives the hormonal imbalances and lipid abnormalities seen in your panel. Addressing insulin resistance through diet, exercise, and targeted supplementation (inositol, berberine) is central to improving your overall health picture.

While total and LDL cholesterol are optimal, your HDL at 44 mg/dL is below the recommended 50 mg/dL for females, and triglycerides at 168 mg/dL are elevated. This pattern is characteristic of insulin resistance. Increasing exercise, omega-3 intake, and reducing refined carbohydrates will improve these markers.

Your hs-CRP at 2.1 mg/L indicates elevated systemic inflammation. This is common in PCOS and insulin resistance. Anti-inflammatory dietary changes and omega-3 supplementation will help lower this marker.

Your vitamin D at 22 ng/mL is insufficient — repletion to 40-60 ng/mL will support insulin sensitivity, hormonal balance, and fertility. Vitamin B12 at 310 pg/mL is adequate, which is important to maintain as a vegetarian.

Your hormonal panel shows the characteristic pattern of PCOS: elevated androgens (testosterone total 78 ng/dL, free 8.2 pg/mL, DHEA-S 380 µg/dL), suppressed SHBG (16 nmol/L), elevated LH:FSH ratio (approximately 3:1), and elevated AMH (6.8 ng/mL). These findings are driven by underlying insulin resistance — high insulin suppresses SHBG and stimulates ovarian androgen production. Addressing insulin resistance is the foundation for improving hormonal balance and supporting fertility.
